judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports correctly identify both blocking defects and correctly treat the raw-SQL string as non-exploitable; they are comparable, with Report 2 marginally more thorough.

report_1.evidence: `search_appointments(session, tenant_id, q, status)` receives `tenant_id` as an argument but never uses it. The raw SQL it builds ... has no `tenant_id` predicate at all. ... any authenticated user of any tenant can search and read every other tenant's appointment `notes` field

report_2.evidence: There is no `tenant_id` clause. The `tenant_id` argument is accepted by the function signature (`search_appointments(session, tenant_id, q, status)`) but never referenced in the query body. ... any authenticated user from Tenant A can search and read Tenant B's (and every other tenant's) appointment records

report_1.evidence: `backend/app/exports.py::nightly_export` — which this PR does not touch — still calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id).all()` with only two arguments. This will raise `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'` on every invocation, i.e. **the nightly per-tenant CSV export job breaks for every tenant**

report_2.evidence: the signature changed to `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id, filters)`, where `filters` is a **required** third positional parameter with no default ... `backend/app/exports.py` is untouched by this PR ... and still calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)` with two arguments at line 9, inside `nightly_export`. This will raise `TypeError`

report_1.evidence: Because the endpoint parameter is typed `status: AppointmentStatus` (a `str` `Enum` with only `booked`/`cancelled`/`completed`), FastAPI/Pydantic validates the value before this function ever sees it, so I don't believe this is currently exploitable for SQL injection.

report_2.evidence: `AppointmentStatus` is a closed `str, Enum` (`booked`/`cancelled`/`completed`), and FastAPI/Pydantic rejects any request where the `status` query parameter isn't one of those literal values before the handler runs — so this is not exploitable *today*.
